cue: When the sum is under target, every partner between the two cursors also qualifies; add them all at once.
Two pointers on the sorted array; a small sum counts a whole block, a large sum shrinks the high end.
prompt: Write `countPairsLess(a, target)` where `a` is sorted ascending. Return the number of index pairs `(i, j)` with `i < j` whose values sum to strictly less than `target`. Use two pointers; do not use a nested loop.
note: When `a[lo] + a[hi] < target`, every partner strictly between lo and hi also qualifies, so you count a whole block at once instead of one pair.
mapsTo: LC 1099 · Two Sum Less Than K

cue: Two pointers from the ends, but slide each past any non-alphanumeric character before comparing.
prompt: Write `isPalAlnum(s)` that returns `true` if `s` reads the same forward and backward when you ignore every non-alphanumeric character and treat upper and lower case as equal. Use two pointers from the ends.

cases.call: isPalAlnum('A man, a plan, a canal: Panama')

cue: Two pointers from the ends; advance a cursor when it is not on a vowel, and swap only when both sit on vowels.
prompt: Write `reverseVowels(s)` that returns `s` with only its vowels reversed and every other character left in place. Vowels are `aeiou` in either case. Use two pointers from the ends.
mapsTo: LC 345 · Reverse Vowels of a String

cue: Opposite ends: leave a correctly placed end alone, and swap only when both ends are misplaced.
Skip a low end that is already negative and a high end that is already non-negative; swap the pair that is out of place.
prompt: Write `partitionSign(a)` that rearranges `a` in place so every negative value comes before every non-negative value, mutating `a` and returning it. Use two pointers from the ends, swapping a misplaced pair. Order within each group need not be preserved.
note: Swapping a misplaced pair from the ends partitions in one pass with O(1) extra space; `.filter` twice would allocate two new arrays.
mapsTo: LC 905 · Sort Array By Parity (partition pattern)

concept: keep at most two of each (sorted)
cue: Read/write compaction, but compare against the value two slots back so each value can survive twice.
prompt: Write `removeDupesTwo(a)` where `a` is sorted ascending. Overwrite `a` in place so each value appears at most twice, keeping order, and return the new length `k`. Use a read pointer and a lagging write pointer.
note: Comparing against `a[w - 2]` instead of `a[w - 1]` is the whole trick: it lets each value survive twice before the third copy is skipped.
mapsTo: LC 80 · Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II

cue: Fix one element, converge two pointers on the rest, and keep the running sum whose distance to target is smallest.

concept: strings are immutable; split to an array to swap
cue: Writing into a character of a string has no effect; only the array copy is mutable.
note: JS strings are immutable, so `s[i] = x` silently does nothing; a two-pointer swap on a string first does `split('')` into an array, then `join('')` back.

concept: three-way partition (sort into three buckets in one pass)
cue: Three cursors: a boundary for 0s, a scanner, and a boundary for 2s; push each scanned value toward its region.
prompt: Write `sortColors(a)` where every entry of `a` is 0, 1, or 2. Sort `a` in place in one pass so all 0s come first, then 1s, then 2s, and return it. Do not use the built-in sort; use three pointers.
note: After swapping a 2 to the back, `mid` does not advance because the value pulled in from `hi` has not been checked yet; a 0 swap comes from `lo`, which is already settled, so `mid` may move.

concept: trapping water: bounded by the shorter side
cue: Two pointers from the ends; the water over the shorter side is fixed by the tallest wall seen so far on that side.
prompt: Write `trap(h)` where `h` is an array of non-negative wall heights. Return the total units of water trapped between the walls after rain. Use two pointers from the ends; do not scan all pairs or build a prefix array.
note: Advancing the shorter side is safe: the water there is capped by that side's running max, since a wall at least as tall is guaranteed on the other side.

cue: Send fast ahead by n links first, then move both one step at a time until fast reaches the last node.
note: With fast held n links ahead, stopping when fast hits the last node leaves slow on the node just before the nth-from-end.

cue: Advance fast n links, then move fast and slow together; slow lands before the node to drop. Handle removing the head.
prompt: Write `removeNth(nxt, start, n)`. The list is an index array where `nxt[i]` is the next index and `-1` is null. Remove the nth node from the end (1-based) by relinking `nxt` in place, and return the index of the new start (it changes only when the head is removed). Use two pointers with a gap of n; do not count the length first.
note: When fast falls off the end after exactly n hops, the node to remove is the head, so the new start is `nxt[start]`; otherwise slow stops on the predecessor and one relink drops the target.
mapsTo: LC 19 · Remove Nth Node From End of List
